coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-supported Zn single atom nanozymes for the catalytic therapy of diabetic wounds

Zhao, Ansha
Yang, Ping
coredata.dc:description:
Diabetic wound treatment continues to be a significant clinical issue due to higher levels of oxidative stress, susceptibility to bacterial infections, and chronic inflammatory responses during healing. We rationally developed and synthesized an ultra-small carbon dots (C-dots) loaded with zinc single-atom nanozyme (Zn/C-dots) with the aim of promoting wounds healing by nanocatalytic treatment, especially targeting its complex pathological microenvironment. Zinc single atoms and C-dots form a dual catalytic system with higher enzymatic activity. Furthermore, the Zn/C-dots nanozyme effectively enters cells, accumulates at mitochondria, and removes excess ROS, protecting cells from oxidative stress damage and limiting the release of pro-inflammatory cytokines, hence reducing inflammation. Zinc can synergistically increase the antibacterial action of C-dots (the effective antibacterial rate of 100 µg/mL Zn/C-dots was above 90 %). Unlike traditional C-dots, Zn/C-dots can cause endothelial cell migration and the formation of new blood vessels. In vitro cytotoxicity, blood compatibility, and in vivo toxicity studies of Zn/C-dots show that they are biocompatible. We subsequently utilized the Zn/C-dots nanozymes to treat diabetic rats' chronic wounds for external use, combining them with ROS-responsive hydrogels to create an antioxidative system (H-Zn/C-dots). The hydrogels anchored the Zn/C-dots nanozymes to the wound, allowing for long-term treatment. The results revealed that H-Zn/C-dots can considerably reduce inflammation, accelerate angiogenesis, collagen deposition, and promote tissue remodeling at the diabetic wound site. After 14 days, the wound area had decreased to approximately 9.19 %, making it a potential treatment.
Statement of significance An ultra-small carbon dot with a zinc single-atom nanozyme was designed and manufactured. Zn/C-dots possess antibacterial, ROS-scavenging, and angiogenesis activities. In vivo, the multifunctional ROS-responsive hydrogel incorporating Zn/C-dots could speed up diabetic wound healing.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of multifunctional hydrogels loaded with Zn/C-dots and a schematic diagram of diabetic wounds repair.

Synthesis and characterization of C-dots and Zn/C-dots:(A) Schematic diagram of Zn/C-dots preparation. (B) Photographs, TEM, and HRTEM images of C-dots and Zn/C-dots%. (C) Particle size statistics of C-dots. (D) Particle size statistics of Zn/C-dots. (E) Zeta potential of C-dots and Zn/C-dots. (F) UV–Vis spectra of C-dots and Zn/C-dots. (G) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of Zn/C-dots (inset: photo graph of Zn/C-dots under bright or UV light). (H) Fluorescence emission spectra of Zn/C-dots under excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 to 420 nm.

(A) FT-IR spectrum of C-dots and Zn/C-dots. XPS survey spectra (B) and high-resolution XPS spectra of Zn2p (C) of C-dots and Zn/C-dots. High-resolution XPS spectra of the C1s (D), O1s (E), and N1s (F) of Zn/C-dots. (G) DPPH clearance of different concentrations of C-dots or Zn/C-dots. (H) UV–VIS spectra of TMB in H2O2, C-dots + H2O2, and Zn/C-dots + H2O2 groups, the inset is the corresponding visual color change. (I) ·O2 − clearance of different concentrations of C-dots or Zn/C-dots. (J) ESR spectra of mixed solutions of X, OX, and DMPO with or without Zn/C-dots. (K) UV–VIS spectra of mixed solutions of Fenton reagent and MB with or without C-dots or Zn/C-dots; the inset is the corresponding visual color change. (L) ESR spectra of mixed solutions of Fenton reagent and MB with or without Zn/C-dots.

Antibacterial activity of C-dots and Zn/C-dots. Representative photos of bacterial colonies co-cultured with E. coli (A) and S. aureus. (B) Statistical results of the antibacterial rate: E. coli (C) and S. aureus (D). (E) SEM image of bacteria.

(A) AO/PI staining of ECs treated with C-dots or Zn/C-dots for 3 days. CCK-8 cell activity tests: 1 day (B), 3 days (C). (D) AO/PI staining of L929 treated with C-dots or Zn/C-dots for 3 days. CCK-8 cell activity tests: 1 day (E), 3 days (F).

(A) Confocal images of the co-localization between Zn/C-dots and the lysosome. (B) Pearson's correlation between Zn/C-dots and the lysosome. (C) Confocal images of the co-localization between Zn/C-dots and mitochondria. (D) Pearson's correlation between Zn/C-dots and mitochondria. Detection of ROS by fluorescence of DCF in the inflammatory cells (LPS-treated MAs) incubated with C-dots and Zn/C-dots: representative fluorescence images (E), mean fluorescence intensity statistics (F), the amount of NO released (G), and the amount of TNF-α released (H).
Migration distance statistics at 12 h (A) and 24 h (B). (C) Representative images of ECs cell migration. (D) Representative images of the tube formation of ECs treated with different groups.

(A) Photographs of the gelation process of a composite hydrogel. (B) SEM images of composite hydrogel. (C) EDS element mapping of hydrogel loaded with Zn/C-dots. (D) LSCM images of hydrogel loaded with C-dots or Zn/C-dots. Antioxidant properties of composite hydrogels: DPPH (E), H2O2 (F), ·O2 − (G), and ·OH (H). (I) Antibacterial properties of composite hydrogels. (J) After 1 day of co-culture of composite hydrogel and HUVECs, the crystal violet staining of HUVECs. (K) Cell count statistics.

(A) Schematic diagram of the diabetes wound-healing model and treatment strategies. (B) Representative images of the diabetic wounds at different times and wound traces of the healing process. (C) Quantification of relative wound area on day 14 after treatment. H&E (D) and masson (E) staining of wound sections in all groups on day 14.

(A) TNF-α immunofluorescence staining of wound sections in all groups on day 3, 7, and 14. Quantification of TNF-α staining: days 3 (B), 7 (C), and 14 (D). (E) CD31 immunofluorescence staining of wound sections in all groups on days 7 and 14. Quantification of CD31 staining: days 7 (F) and 14 (G).

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-supported Zn single atom nanozymes for the catalytic therapy of diabetic wounds

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Diabetic wound treatment continues to be a significant clinical issue due to higher levels of oxidative stress, susceptibility to bacterial infections, and chronic inflammatory responses during healing. We rationally developed and synthesized an ultra-small carbon dots (C-dots) loaded with zinc single-atom nanozyme (Zn/C-dots) with the aim of promoting wounds healing by nanocatalytic treatment, especially targeting its complex pathological microenvironment. Zinc single atoms and C-dots form a dual catalytic system with higher enzymatic activity. Furthermore, the Zn/C-dots nanozyme effectively enters cells, accumulates at mitochondria, and removes excess ROS, protecting cells from oxidative stress damage and limiting the release of pro-inflammatory cytokines, hence reducing inflammation. Zinc can synergistically increase the antibacterial action of C-dots (the effective antibacterial rate of 100 µg/mL Zn/C-dots was above 90 %). Unlike traditional C-dots, Zn/C-dots can cause endothelial cell migration and the formation of new blood vessels. In vitro cytotoxicity, blood compatibility, and in vivo toxicity studies of Zn/C-dots show that they are biocompatible. We subsequently utilized the Zn/C-dots nanozymes to treat diabetic rats' chronic wounds for external use, combining them with ROS-responsive hydrogels to create an antioxidative system (H-Zn/C-dots). The hydrogels anchored the Zn/C-dots nanozymes to the wound, allowing for long-term treatment. The results revealed that H-Zn/C-dots can considerably reduce inflammation, accelerate angiogenesis, collagen deposition, and promote tissue remodeling at the diabetic wound site. After 14 days, the wound area had decreased to approximately 9.19 %, making it a potential treatment.

An ultra-small carbon dot with a zinc single-atom nanozyme was designed and manufactured. Zn/C-dots possess antibacterial, ROS-scavenging, and angiogenesis activities. In vivo, the multifunctional ROS-responsive hydrogel incorporating Zn/C-dots could speed up diabetic wound healing.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes has become a major global health concern, with an estimated 19–34 % of diabetic patients suffering from chronic non-healing wounds and other severe and expensive complications that endanger their health and lives [1-4]. Normal wound healing consists mostly of four programmed processes: hemostasis, inflammation, proliferation, and re-epithelialization [5]. However, chronic diabetes wounds are likely to remain in an inflammatory state indefinitely [6-8]. Chronic, non-healing diabetic wounds are caused by a variety of factors, including excessive ROS accumulation, bacterial infection, and long-term inflammation. Furthermore, low blood vessel formation caused by high blood sugar restricts nutrition and oxygen delivery to the wound site, delaying wound healing [8]. The high-sugar environment provided ideal breeding conditions for bacteria, resulting in long-term bacterial infections [9,10]. As a result, the development of multifunctional materials and the improvement of the complex microenvironment mentioned earlier are critical for diabetic wound healing.

The aberrant formation of ROS has been elucidated as a key mediator of inflammatory pathophysiology, with high ROS levels frequently resulting in irreversible local oxidative damage and increased inflammation [11-13]. Eliminating excessive ROS from diabetic wounds is a promising method for overcoming wound inflammation and healing stasis [14]. Under normal conditions, the body's naturally occurring antioxidants, such as superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT), and glutathione peroxidase (GPx), regulate ROS. Most natural enzymes face challenges such as unpredictability, high cost, complicated preparation, and severe burdens of large-scale manufacturing [15]. Nanoparticles with natural enzymatic activity have made significant breakthroughs in biomedical engineering due to their stability and high catalytic activity. Single-atom nanoenzymes (SANs) with atomically dispersed metal cores exhibit the greatest inherent enzymatic activity [16-18]. Carbon compounds are widely used as ideal carriers for single-atom catalysis due to their unique structure and stable physicochemical properties. When carbon materials are utilized as carriers, the surface single atoms influence the carrier's local electronic structure, resulting in strong metal-carrier interactions that change the spatial state and energy distribution of adsorbates while also enhancing the catalyst's catalytic performance. However, generating single metal atoms on carbon carriers is still difficult.

Recently, carbon dots (C-dots) have sparked significant research interest in biomedicine due to their small size, ease of production, low cost, and good biocompatibility [19-22]. Carbon dots' size effect and abundance of active sites provide enzyme-like catalytic activity, such as superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT), and others [23,24]. Importantly, recent studies have shown that carbon dots are good super supports for SANs due to their huge specific surface area and abundance of surface anchoring sites. SANs can form a dual catalytic system with C-dots and demonstrate increased enzymatic activity, indicating enormous promise for nanocatalytic therapy [25-27].

Rapid advances in nanotechnology offer new options for combating bacterial infections. Five classes of antimicrobial nanomaterials were widely reported, including metallic NPs (e.g., Ag, Cu, Au, ZnO, La2O3, CeO2, V2O5, etc.), carbonaceous nanomaterials (e.g., GO, graphene, carbon nanotube (CNT)), borides (e.g., BN), nanosized polymers (e.g., polycarbonate), and nanocomposites (e.g., La2O3/Ag-GO) have all been extensively studied [28,29]. Carbon dots have been shown to reduce biofilm formation by reducing bacterial adhesion, disrupting quorum sensing and killing bacteria, making them a promising option for a new generation of antimicrobial treatments [30]. Zn ions have been shown to significantly angiogenesis, activate genes associated with skin healing, drive endothelial cell proliferation and migration, and promote vascularization of early injured regions [31]. Zn ions are also an important trace element in hair follicle development, and wound dressings containing Zn can significantly increase the creation of hair follicle tissue at the wound site [32]. As a result, it is worthwhile to do additional research on selecting metal Zn monodispersed loaded on carbon dots in order to manufacture the zinc single-atom nanozymes.

Based on the above research, we synthesized a zinc single-atom nanozyme (Zn/C-dots) supported by ultrasmall carbon dots (C-dots) with catalytic, antibacterial, angiogenic, and anti-inflammatory properties that can aid in diabetic wounds (Scheme 1 ). First, we produced Zn/C-dots by using a one-step hydrothermal technique and investigated their structural traits, physicochemical properties, and ROS scavenging capacity. The appropriate concentration of Zn/C-dots was then determined using in vitro antibacterial experiments, cytotoxicity assessments, blood compatibility tests, and in vivo toxicity trials. Furthermore, the ultra-small size and fluorescence properties of Zn/C-dots were used to investigate their cellular uptake and subcellular accumulation. The anti-inflammatory effectiveness of Zn/C-dots will be evaluated in vitro using an oxidative stress paradigm. Finally, we developed a boronic ester-based microenvironment-responsive hydrogel as a carrier to carry Zn/C-dots and investigated the potential of Zn/C-dots as nano-catalytic agents for the treatment of diabetic wounds.

Citric acid, guanidine hydrochloride, zinc chloride (ZnCl2), NaOH (0.1 M) standard solution, Diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl radical 2,2-Diphenyl-1-(2,4,6-trinitrophenyl) hydroxyl (DPPH), carbinol, methylene blue, and iron (II) chloride (FeCl2) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Co. (Shanghai, China). A dialysis bag (500 Da) was bought from MYM Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Hangzhou, China). 1,1-TMB two-component substrate solution (for Elisa) was purchased from Bioss Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Hydrogen peroxide solution (H2O2, 35 %) was purchased from Jingmen Shuangxiong Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Hubei, China). Neotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT), Xanthine, Xanthine oxidase, Acridine Orange (AO), Propidium iodine (PI), 2′,7′-Dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), Lipopolysaccharide (LPS), and Polyvinyl Alcohol (PVA, 23 kDa) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Cell Counting Kit-8 was purchased from Biosharp Technology Ltd. (Anhui, China). Matrix-Gel was obtained from Beyotime Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). Hyaluronic acid (HA, 50 WDa) was bought from Bloomage Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shandong, China), and phenylboric acid (PBA) was purchased from Alfa Aesar Co. (Tianjin, China). Streptozocin (STZ) was purchased from Solarbio Technology Co., Ltd.

1 g citric acid and 2 g guanidine hydrochloride were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized (DI) water, followed by the addition of (20 mg) or no ZnCl2, and the pH was adjusted to 11 with the NaOH standard solution. The solution was moved to a high-pressure reactor, where it reacted for 6 h at 160 °C. After cooling to room temperature, use a 200 µm filter membrane to remove bigger particles. This was followed by dialysis with a 500 D dialysis bag to remove the unreacted molecules. Finally, C-dots and Zn/C-dots were obtained by freeze-drying.

Using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JSM-2100F, JEOL, Japan) to observe the morphologies of C-dots and Zn/C-dots. The Zeta potential was obtained by a highly sensitive Zeta potential and particle size analyzer (Nano ZS, Malvern, Britain). Ultraviolet absorption spectra and photoluminescence (PL, F4600, Hitachi, Japan) spectra were obtained by using ultraviolet spectrophotometers (UV–Vis, UV-3600 plus, Shimadzu, Japan) and fluorescence spectrophotometers (FS5, Edinburgh Instruments, UK). Using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; K-Alpha, Thermo Electron, USA), we analyzed the elemental composition of C-dots and Zn/C-dots. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy was obtained by a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Nicolet 5700FT-IP, Thermo Electron Corporation, America).

The DPPH powder was produced to 0.04 mg/mL with methanol and correspondingly supplemented with different concentrations of C-dots or Zn/C-dots (final concentrations of 0, 50, 100, 200, and 500 µg/mL, respectively). After reacting for 5 min in the dark, the solution was absorbed for an absorbance test (517 nm) to determine the DPPH clearance.

The pod-like activity of C-dots and Zn/C-dots was evaluated using TMB as a substrate. Specifically, 30 µL C-dots or Zn/C-dots (10 mg/mL) solution, 100 µL H2O2 (35 %), 300 µL TMB A, and 300 µL TMB solution were added into 700 µL phosphate buffer (PBS, pH 6.8). The reaction was allowed to settle for 6 h before being evaluated for UV absorption.

The superoxide anion (·O2 −) was produced by mixing xanthine (0.6 mM) with xanthine oxidase (0.05 U/mL). Different concentrations of C-dots or Zn/C-dots were added in proportion (0, 50, 100, 200, and 500 µg/mL), followed by NBT (1.6 mg/mL) for color reaction. Finally, the solution was collected for the absorbance test (550 nm), and the ·O2 − clearance rate was calculated.

In situ electron spin resonance (ESR) was used to further verify the SOD-like activity of Zn/C-dots. Specifically, ESR was used to detect ·O2 − in a solution of 1 mL X, 10 µL XO, and 1 mL Zn/C-dots after a 5-minute reaction.

OH was produced by mixing H2O2 and Fe2+. 500 µL DI water was treated for five minutes with the addition of 10 µL FeCl2 (8 mg/mL) and 10 µL H2O2 (35 %). Following a 5-minute reaction, 1 mL of methylene blue solution (MB, 0.1 mg/mL) was added, followed by C-dots or Zn/C-dots. The UV absorption spectrum was tested after the MB color faded.

ESR was used to further validate the ability of Zn/C-dots to clear ·OH. FeCl2, H2O2, and Zn/C-dots (1:1:2 mixing). Following a five-minute reaction, ·OH was detected by ESR and captured using 5‑tert-butoxycarbonyl 5-methyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (BMPO).

Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S.aureus) were used as research objects. First, we adjusted the number of bacteria to 108 CFU/mL, Then, 100 µL C-dots or Zn/C-dots (0, 0.5, 1, 2, and 5 mg/mL) were added to 900 µL the bacteria suspension (penicillin was used as a negative control). The mixture was then cultured for 18 h at 37 °C. The mixed bacterial solution was diluted by four orders of magnitude using the conventional continuous dilution procedure. A 50 µL culture sample was evenly spread on an AGAR plate and incubated at 37 °C for 15 h. The number of bacterial colonies was photographed and calculated.

The previous bacterial solution was co-cultured with C-dots or Zn/C-dots. After centrifugation, the bacteria were collected and preserved in a paraformaldehyde solution for 12 h. Centrifuging again to remove the paraformaldehyde. After dehydration using an ethanol gradient, the bacterial droplets were applied to the Si sheet, followed by drying, gold spraying, and SEM inspection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and cytotoxicity evaluation in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Mouse fibroblasts (L929) and human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) were obtained from Servicebio (Wuhan, China) for cell cytotoxicity evaluation. In a 48-well cell culture plate, added 500 µL of DMEM/F12 in 10 % fetal bovine serum (containing cell suspension (2 × 105 cell/mL)), followed by C-dots or Zn/C-dots at a final concentration of 0, 50, 100, 200, and 500 µg/mL. After 1, 3 days of culture (5 % CO2, 37 °C), CCK-8 and AO/PI live/dead staining were used to assess the cytotoxicity of C-dots and Zn/C-dots. The AO/PI live/dead staining procedure was as follows: first, 10 µL AO (100 mg/mL) and 10 µL PI (100 mg/mL) were added to 1 mL cultured media, then incubated in the dark for 3 min, cleaned with normal saline, and photographed using fluorescence microscopy.

HUVECs were inoculated in a 24-well cell culture plate. After fully coating the cells with a single layer, a pipette head (200 µL) was used to scrape the center of the hole plate, followed by 10 µL of C-dots or Zn/C-dots (10 mg/mL). To analyze cell migration, the cells were cultivated for 12 and 24 h before being imaged under a microscope.

The matrigel matrix was dissolved at 4 °C, 50 µL of Matrigel matrix was added to the pre-cooled 96-well plate under sterile conditions, and it was then incubated at 37 °C for 2 h to produce a gel. Add 100 µL of HUVECs suspension (30 × 105 cells/mL) on a plate precoated with Matrigel matrix, followed by 10 µL of C-dots or Zn/C-dots (1 mg/mL). After 3 h of culture, the tube formation was analyzed under a microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Macrophages cultured and inflammatory factor detection
ce:section.ce:para: Macrophages (MAs, Raw264.7) were bought from Servicebio (Wuhan, China). The 48-well cell culture plate was filled with a 500 µL cell suspension (8 × 105 cell/mL) containing DMEM in 10 % fetal bovine serum, C-dots or Zn/C-dots were then added at final concentrations of 0, 50, 100, 200, and 500 µg/mL. CCK-8 was used to assess cell viability after 1–3 days of culture in a cell incubator (5 % CO2, 37 °C), Which was evaluated by the NO analyzer (NOA, Seivers 280i, Boulder, CO) monitored MAs' NO release while an ELISA kit (KeyGEN BioTECH) evaluated the release of the inflammatory cytokine TNF-α.

The cellular uptake of MAs cells was examined using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). MAs cells were seeded in 48-well culture plates with a density of 5 × 104 cells per well and adhere for 24 h. The cells were treated with Zn/C-dots (100 µg/mL) for varying durations. Following that, the supernatant was carefully removed, and the cells were washed three times with PBS. Subsequently, the cells were observed with a confocal laser scanning microscope imaging system. Lyso-Tracker (green) was used to track the location of lysosome. Mito-Tracker (green) was used to track the location of mitochondria.

MAs were inoculated into a 48-well cell culture plate, then treated with 1 µg/mL lipopolysaccharide (LPS) for 24 h. C-dots or Zn/C-dots (final concentration of 100 µg/mL) were added. After 24 h of treatment, cells were stained with reactive oxygen species (ROS) fluorescent probe dichlorofluorescein (DCFH-DA). CLSM and image J were used to analyze the intracellular ROS levels, and finally, the NO analyzer detected MAs' NO release.

All animal experiments were in accordance with the protocols approved by the Animal Care and Use Committee of Southwest Jiaotong University. Animals were died by air embolization after use. The blood compatibility of C-dots and Zn/C-dots was evaluated by a hemolysis test. Blood from healthy rats was collected through a negative pressure tube with anticoagulant, and 4 mL of blood was added to a 5 mL NaCl solution. 200 µL of the above blood and 100 µL of C-dots or Zn/C-dots (10 mg/mL) were added to 9.7 mL NaCl solution (DI water, as a positive control), incubated at 37 °C for 1 h, centrifuged at 3000 r/min for 5 min, and 150 µL of solution were taken for the absorbance test (540 nm). Finally, the hemolysis rate was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Organ toxicity and blood biochemistry tests
ce:section.ce:para: Sprague-Dawley (SD) rats (300 g, male) were used for organ toxicity and blood biochemistry tests. The rats were anesthetized with 20 % ulaiose (800 mg/kg) before received a single injection of 1 mL of C-dots or Zn/C-dots solution (2.4 mg/mL) though their tail vein. After 7 days, the rats were anesthetized and their blood was collected for biochemical analysis (ALT, AST, uric acid, CREA, and creatine kinase). Organ tissues were collected and fixed with paraformaldehyde following NaCl cleaning, followed by paraffin embedding and tissue sections were performed, and hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining was used to evaluate the biosafety of C-dots and Zn/C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of hydrogels loaded with Zn/C-dots
Preparation of hydrogels loaded with Zn/C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The hyaluronic acid modified by phenylboric acid (HA-PBA) was prepared according to the work of Li et al. [33], and then HA-PBA was mixed with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) in a specific proportion to prepare HA-PBA-PVA hydrogel by forming a dynamic bond of boron lipid. Specifically, by mixing 2.5 Wt.% HA-PBA with 2.5 Wt.% PVA at room temperature at a 3:1 volume ratio, a certain proportion of C-dots or Zn/C-dots was added during the preparation of HA-PBA-PVA hydrogels (the final C-dots or Zn/C-dots concentration was 100 µg/mL) to prepare hydrogels loaded with C-dots (HC-dots) or Zn/C-dots (H-Zn/C-dots).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of hydrogels loaded with Zn/C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: H-Zn/C-dots were immersed in liquid nitrogen and then broken, and the microstructure of H-Zn/C-dots was examined by SEM following gold spraying. The distribution of Zn/C-dots in hydrogels was analyzed using energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) and laser confocal microscopy. The tourier transform infrared spectroscopy was performed using an FT-IR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS scavenging performance of composite hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: Refer to Section 2.4 for ROS scavenging experiments of composite hydrogels.
Refer to Section 2.5 for antibacterial experiments of composite hydrogels.

A rotating rheometer with flat shape (DHR-Ⅱ, TA Instruments) was used at 37 °C to perform oscillatory frequency scanning (from 0.01 to 100 Hz) and strain amplitude scanning (from 0.1 % to 1000 %). The energy storage modulus, loss modulus, and self-healing behavior of hydrogels were investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug release behavior of complex hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The composite hydrogel was placed in a dialysis bag and placed on PBS (pH 5.5, 6.8, 7.4, 5.5 + H2O2 (100 µM)), Within the predetermined period (0/2/4/8/24/48 h), 200 µL solution was collected and replaced fresh solution with equal-volume. Ultimately, a UV spectrophotometer was used to calculated the drug release rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell compatibility of hydrogels loaded with Zn/C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The cell compatibility of hydrogels loaded with Zn/C-dots was assessed using the chemotaxis chamber method. To begin, a layer of HC-dots or H-Zn/C-dots was deposited in the 24-well cell culture plate, cell media was then added, followed by the chemotactic chamber and cell suspension (2 × 104 cells/mL) in the upper chamber. Incubating in a cell incubator for 24 h. The chemotactic chamber was removed, and the upper cells were gently wiped off with a cotton swab before being fixed in paraformaldehyde for 10 min. After cleaning, the cells were stained with 0.25 % crystal violet for 10 min. Finally, the cells were observed using a microscope, photographed, and counted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial properties of composite hydrogels in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: The composite hydrogel's antibacterial properties were evaluated in vivo using a wound infection model. Before surgery, the rats were sedated with an intraperitoneal injection of 20 % ulailose. A hair remover was used to shave the rat's back hair and make a 10 mm incision in the back. Next, 20 µL of Staphylococcus aureus (1 × 108 CFU/mL) was put on the wound site to simulate infection. The hydrogel was subsequently placed on the wound, and two days later, skin samples from the infected area were obtained and washed with 1 mL of sterile PBS. The 50 µL solution was uniformly coated on the solid medium. They were cultivated at 37 °C for 24 h and then photographed for analysis. Skin samples were embedded, sectioned, and analyzed by Gram staining.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Skin wound healing in diabetic model rats

ce:section.ce:para: The diabetes model was based on the literature, with few modifications [34,35]. Sprague Dawley (SD) rats (300-350 g, male) were used as model rats. All rats were fed an adapted diet for 1 week and then intraperitoneally injected with streptozotocin (STZ, 60 mg/kg) after a 12-hour fast. Fasting blood glucose was measured three times over five days at random; if it was higher than 11.1 mmol/L, the diabetes model was successful. If the blood glucose did not reach 11.1 mmol/L, the appeal procedure was repeated until the blood glucose was higher than 11.1 mmol/L.

After anesthetizing the rats, a sterile hole punch was used to create a full-layer skin wound with a diameter of 1 cm on their back. Then, add 100 µL of H-Zn/C-dots to completely cover the wound. Wound pictures were collected at 3, 5, 7, 9, and 14 days for each group, and the wound area was statistically analyzed.

At 3, 7, and 14 days, tissues from the wounds were removed, cleaned with NaCl, and fixed with paraformaldehyde. To observe skin regeneration, collagen deposition, neovasculation, and inflammation, paraffin embedding, tissue sections, hematoxylin and eosin (H&E), Masson, and immunohistochemical (CD31 and TNF-α) staining were performed after that.

All experiments were conducted in triplicate. The data was analyzed using Graphpad Prism and Origin Pro and displayed as the mean ± standard deviation. A one-way ANOVA test was used for comparison between the two groups. When the P-value is below 0.05 (p < 0.05 *,p < 0.01 **,p < 0.001 ***), it is considered significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of C-dots and Zn/C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: C-dots and Zn/C-dots were synthesized using small organic molecules as carbon sources in “bottom-up” synthesis [36-39]. Briefly, C-dots were synthesized by directly pyrolyzing guanidine hydrochloride and citric acid using the-one step hydrothermal method (Fig. 1A). Similarly, Zn2+ was added to the above solution in hydrothermal processes, yielding Zn/C-dots. C-dots and Zn/C-dots were yellow transparent liquids (Fig. 1B), which were be filtered and dialyzed to remove impurities. TEM images demonstrated that C-dots and Zn/C-dots were monodisperse, quasi-spherical nanoparticles with a lattice space of 0.21 nm to the (100) facet of graphite [40]. The average size of C-dots was calculated to be 4.00 ± 0.1 nm, and the Zn/C-dots were 3.34 ± 0.25 nm (Fig. 1C and D), which was less than the kidney filtration threshold (5.5 nm) and microvasculature vessel diameters (arterioles 10–100 µm, capillaries 4–10 µm, and venules 10–100 µm [41,42]. Moreover, their zeta potential changed from −35.06 ± 2.99 mV (C-dots) to −31.60 ± 1.27 mV (Zn/C-dots) (Fig. 1E). The optical characteristics of C-dots and Zn/C-dots were subsequently evaluated. The UV–VIS absorption spectrum (Fig. 1F) revealed strong absorption bands at 237 nm and 344 nm, which corresponded to π → π* and n → π* transitions [43], The red shift of the 344 nm band after Zn doping could be attributed to the formation of a larger conjugated system. Moreover, C-dots and Zn/C-dots also showed obvious fluorescence characteristics (Fig. S1 and Fig. 1G). The Zn/C-dots emitted at 447 nm when the ideal excitation wavelength was 355 nm. Zn/C-dots exhibited typical excitation-dependent emission (Fig. 1H), which was widely seen in reported carbon dots.

The FT-IR absorption spectra (Fig. 2A) showed that several strong absorption bands at 1078, 1415, 1581, and 3426 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of CO, CO, CN, and OH/NH bonds. These hydrophilic groups enhanced Zn/C-dots good stability and biocompatibility in the water [44,45]. In addition, the characteristic peak at 964 cm−1 corresponds to the ZnN bond, indicating chemical coordination between Zn and C-dots [41,46]. Chemical element composition and chemical state in C-dots and Zn/C-dots were analyzed using XPS (Fig. 2B and Table S1), C-dots were mainly composed of C, N, and O elements, whereas Zn/C-dots were mainly composed of C, N, O, and Zn elements, with an atomic percentage of Zn was 1.58 % (Fig. 2C). The Zn2p spectrum (Fig. S2) revealed four mains’ peaks associated with Zn0 and Zn2+, which serve as the structural foundation for the enzymatic reactions. The C1s spectrum of Zn/C-dots (Fig. 2D) showed four main peaks, with the peaks at 284.88, 285.53, 287.03, and 288.68 eV assigned to CC, CC, COC, and OCO [47]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2E) contained four main peaks, which were assigned to ZnO, CO, COC, and COH [48]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2F) showed two main peaks, 398.78 and 399.48 eV, which were attributed to pyridinic and pyrrolic N structures [49,50]. FTIR and XPS studies showed that Zn/C-dots surfaces were rich in a large number of functional group structures, and Zn atoms were successfully doped through chemical coordination of ZnN and ZnO (Fig. S4). The atomic structure and doping of active sites are major factors in catalytic reaction performance [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enzyme-like activity of C-dots and Zn/C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The Antioxidant activities of C-dots and Zn/C-dots were evaluated using the DPPH radical scavenging method. Fig. 2G showed that both C-dots and Zn/C-dots exhibited obvious DPPH scavenging ability, with Zn/C-dots having a greater DPPH scavenging ability at the same concentration. The POD-like activities of C-dots and Zn/C-dots were studied by using the substrate TMB. Previously research has shown that the colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) can be catalyzed to the blueness of oxidized TMB (OX-TMB) in the presence of H2O2 and POD-like mimics. Fig. 2H revealed that in the presence of Zn/C-dots, the mixed solution (TMB and H2O2) turned a distinct blue color (illustration), and the characteristic absorption peak of OX-TMB (650 nm) was found. These results indicated that Zn/C-dots exhibited clear POD-like activity. The SOD-like activity of C-dots and Zn/C-dots was evaluated using NBT, which reacts with ·O2 − and produce products with absorption peaks at 550 nm. Zn/C-dots had stronger SOD-like activity than C-dots, with a positive correlation between concentration and ·O2 − clearance (Fig. 2I). The SOD-like activity of Zn/C-dots was further evaluated using ESR spectroscopy. DMPO may capture·O2 − to generate DMPO/·OOH. As shown in Fig. 2J, Zn/C-dots greatly reduced the ESR signal of DMPO/·OOH, showing good ·O2 − scavenging activities, agreeing with the results of the NBT test results. C-dots and Zn/C-dots exhibit effective ·OH scavenging activity. MB with a peak absorbance of 665 nm could be oxidized as colorless under the action of ·OH (·OH was produced by the reaction of H2O2 and Fe2+). As shown in Fig. 2K, the absorption peak of MB + H2O2 + Fe2+ groups decreased significantly, indicating that MB was significantly degraded during oxidation. In the presence of C-dots and Zn/C-dots, the absorption peak decreased only little, indicating that ·OH was cleared. ESR results (Fig. 2L) demonstrated that Zn/C-dots effectively removed ·OH. The above results indicated Zn/C-dots possessed ROS scavenging abilities, and the addition of Zn active sites significantly increased the catalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activities of C-dots and Zn/C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Gram-positive bacteria (S.ureus) and negative bacteria (E.coli) were used to evaluate the antibacterial activity of C-dots and Zn/C-dots using the live cell counting method [52,53]. As shown in Figs. 3A and C, C-dots only show exceptional anti-E.coli performance when their concentration is higher than 500 µg/mL. Differentially, in the presence of 100 µg/mL Zn/C-dots, the antibacterial rate of E.coli can exceed 90%. For S.aureus, Zn/C-dots showed over 90% antibacterial activity at 50 µg/mL, while C-dots required 200 µg/mL (Fig. 3B and D). SEM results (Fig. 3E) showed that the bacteria in the positive group still retained the typical spherical or rod shape, with the surface was intact and smooth. In contrast, bacteria (E. coli and S. aureus) treated with Zn/C-dots experienced substantial membrane deformation, as reflected by the shrinkage, denaturation, and rupture of cell membranes, causing cytoplasm to flow out. C-dots showed a strong attraction for the peptidoglycan (PGN) and lipopolysaccharide (LPS) in bacterial cell walls. This allows them to partially disrupt and destroy the structure of the bacterial membrane, resulting in extremely effective bacterial death [54]. In addition, the synergistic effect of Zn can also improve antibacterial activity.

Good antibacterial activity can kill bacteria while preventing wound infection; theoretically, the best antibacterial rate must reach more than 90% to prevent wound infection [54,55]. Bacterial experiments showed that Zn/C-dots had acceptable antibacterial activity at 100 µg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro biocompatibility of C-dots and Zn/C-dots
Culture of vascular endothelial cells (HUVECs) and fibroblast cells (L929) in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: L929 cells and HUVECs play critical roles in skin wound angiogenesis and tissue healing. Thus, by co-culturing with cells, the effects of C-dots and Zn/C-dots on cell proliferation were assessed. after one day of co-cultivation with HUVECs and L929, none of the C-dots or Zn/C-dots displayed cytotoxicity, and the CCK-8 test and AO/PI staining results showed no detectable differences (Fig. 4B and E). Zn/C-dots were able to promote cell proliferation in HUVECs after three days of co-cultivation (Fig. 4A and C). Regarding L929, it was seen that Zn/C-dots and C-dots could both stimulate the growth of L929 (Fig. 4D and F). However, it was important to note that cell viability peaked at 100 µg/mL Zn/C-dots and then drastically declined, resulting cell growth. An appropriate amount of zinc can speed up the creation and remodeling of the extracellular matrix as well as encourage fibroblast migration and the conversion of fibroblasts into myofibroblasts [56,57].

Here, we investigated the effects of Zn/C-dots at various concentrations on MAs. As shown in Fig. S5, Fig. S6 A and B, it was discovered that increasing the concentration of C-dots considerably inhibited MAs growth, and the inhibition of MAs was more intense at the same concentration of Zn/C-dots. C-dots and Zn/C-dots above 200 µg/mL resulted in significantly higher levels of NO [58] released by cells (Fig. S6 C). High concentrations of C-dots and Zn/C-dots may cause inflammation. Therefore, a concentration of 100 µg/mL was selected for further research on anti-inflammatory ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular endocytosis and subcellular localization of Zn/C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of Zn/C-dots to enter living cells was investigated. After incubation with Zn/C-dots (100 µg/mL) for various durations, cells' uptake was monitored using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). As shown in Fig. S7, Zn/C-dots were largely accumulated on the cell surface during the 5–60-minute co-incubation period. As time progressed (2–4 h), the number of C-dots entering the cells rose steadily and were largely localized in the cytoplasm. Additionally, we investigated the subcellular localization of Zn/C-dots. Lysosomes are the main organelles responsible for phagocytosing exogenous substances, whereas mitochondria are the primary organelles that generate ROS and induce cellular oxidative damage. Therefore, we examined whether the carbon dots entered these two organelles. Lysosomes and mitochondria were stained using Lyso-Tracker and Mito-Tracker, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5A and B, Zn/C-dots demonstrated in lysosomal accumulation (Pearson's correlation was 0.564). Simultaneously, accumulation of Zn/C-dots was also observed in mitochondria (Pearson's correlation was 0.893) (Fig. 5C and D), which is favorable for the scavenging of ROS, especially those generated in mitochondria, such as ·O2 −.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-inflammatory activities of C-dots and Zn/C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Excess ROS would cause lipid peroxidation, protein denaturation, and DNA damage, resulting in a variety of illnesses. Numerous nanozymes have been employed to remove harmful ROS in vitro and in vivo [33]. The intracellular antioxidant capacity of C-dots was investigated using MAs as an example.

MAs were treated with LPS to establish research models with relatively high levels of ROS [58]. As demonstrated in Fig. 5E and F, the control group had a weak fluorescence signal, indicating normal ROS level. While cells treated with LPS showed high fluorescence intensity, displaying an upregulated ROS level. In contrast, C-dots and Zn/C-dots dramatically reduced the fluorescence intensity of LPS-treated cells, indicating the ROS-scavenging capacity of C-dots in living cells. Tumor necrosis factor TNF-α, and NO were the biomarkers of acute inflammation. The C-dots significantly decreased the levels of NO (Fig. 5G) and TNF-α (Fig. 5H) in cells treated with LPS.
The above results showed that a certain concentration of C-dots and Zn/C-dots could clear ROS and inhibit cell inflammatory response.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HUVECs cell migration and tube formation in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Migration and tube formation of HUVECs are important ways to evaluate the angiogenesis activity of materials in vitro. As shown in Fig. 6A-C, both C-dots and Zn/C-dots (100 µg/mL) promoted HUVECs migration, especially Zn/C-dots. Zn/C-dots demonstrated a strong capacity to stimulate angiogenesis in an in vitro tubule formation experiment (Fig. 6D), which demonstrated that the dots could greatly promote tube formation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosafety evaluation of C-dots and Zn/C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The favorable biocompatibility is the primary prerequisite for the in vivo application of biomaterials. Firstly, the blood compatibility of C-dots and Zn/C-dots was evaluated. With the exception of the positive group, the hemolysis test (Fig. S8) showed that all samples’ supernatants were transparent and that normal blood cells were deposited at the bottom. Every test sample had a hemolysis rate of less than 5 %, indicating satisfactory good blood safety.

The biosafety of C-dots and Zn/C-dots in vivo was then evaluated in healthy SD rats. following a solitary injection (2.4 mg, 1 mL, equal to 200 µg per 1 mL of blood). As shown in Figure Fig. S9A, there was no necrosis, congestion, inflammatory lesions, or tissue damage in the heart, kidney, liver, lung, or spleen. At the same time, no abnormality was found in the results of blood biochemical tests (ALT, AST, UA, CREA, CK) (Fig. S9B). The results showed that C-dots and Zn/C-dots showed good biosafety in vivo.

Following the validation of Zn/C-dots' catalytic, antibacterial, angiogenic, anti-inflammatory properties, and biosafety characteristics, we proceeded to investigate their potential as nano catalytic drugs in the treatment of diabetic wounds. Hydrogels are widely used as wound dressings because they can absorb wound exudates, maintain moisture at the wound site, and have a suitable modulus that matches soft tissue. Its porous form also makes it an ideal drug carrier; in particular, biodegradable hydrogels with special ROS-responsive qualities make them great options for both sustained release and local drug administration [59,60]. Therefore, we synthesized ROS-responsive hydrogel H-Zn/C-dots by dissolving Zn/C-dots in HA-PBA and PVA solution for external usage. This allowed us to deliver the Zn/C-dots nanozymes to diabetic wounds and effectively retain the Zn/C-dots nanozymes in the wound tissue.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of hydrogels loaded with Zn/C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of hydrogels composed of hyaluronic acid modified with phenylboronic acid (HA-PBA) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) for biomedical applications was facilitated by the incorporation of C-dots or Zn/C-dots during the gelation process, yielding composite hydrogels loaded with C-dots or Zn/C-dots. Crucially, the hydrogel's gelation process was unaffected by the presence of C- and Zn/C-dots (Fig. 7 A). The characterization of the internal structure of the freeze-dried hydrogel was characterized by SEM (Fig. 7B), which revealed a loose and porous network that was conducive to the rapid absorption of blood and tissue exudates at the wound site. The homogeneous distribution of C-dots and Zn/C-dots inside the hydrogel matrix was shown by EDS elemental mapping for C, N, and Zn (Fig. 7C) and 3D fluorescence imaging of C-dots and Zn/C-dots by laser confocal microscopy (Fig. 7D).

The hydrogel dressing's self-healing characteristics can help prevent functional failure caused by structural damage. When two hydrogels are placed together and contacted for 10 min, they can recombine to form new hydrogels that extended to both sides (Fig. S11 A), suggesting outstanding self-healing and elastic capabilities. Rheological tests were performed to future observe the self-healing behavior of hydrogels (Fig. S11 B). The hydrogels retained elastic characteristics across the test frequency range, and (G') regularly exceeded (G"). Zn/C-dot loading had no significant influence on hydrogel self-healing or storage modulus. This suggested that Zn/C-dots were primarily physically loaded in hydrogels, and infrared data (Fig. S11 C) corroborated this hypothesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug release behavior of complex hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the response of the hydrogel's borate ester bond to pH and H2O2, the drug release behavior of the composite hydrogel in various pH and H2O2 environments was investigated further. As shown in Fig. S12, ZnC/dot can be released continuously for 48 h. Finally, the release rate was 65.58 % in PBS with pH 5 + H2O2, 57.7 % in PBS with pH 5.5, 53.8 % in PBS with pH 6.7, and 50.7 % in PBS with pH 7.4. Wounds are frequently acidic, often accompanied by the production of highly reactive oxygen species, and these results suggest that hydrogels can rapidly respond to the wound microenvironment and deliver drugs sequentially.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant and antibacterial properties of composite hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The ROS scavenging efficiency of composite hydrogels including Zn/C-dots was further investigated, as illustrated in Fig. 7E-H. Due to the ROS reactivity of the borate ester bond, HA-PBA/PVA displayed ROS scavenging capabilities. The ROS clearance ability was further enhanced by the inclusion of C-dots or Zn/C-dots. In addition, in vitro antibacterial studies demonstrated that H-Zn/C-dots exhibited outstanding antibacterial capabilities in vitro (Fig. 7I).

Finally, the biocompatibility of the composite hydrogel was evaluated, with the results of the transwell migration assay (Fig. 7 J and K) demonstrating enhanced migration of HUVECs in the C-dots and Zn/C-dots-loaded hydrogel compared to the pure hydrogel, indicating good cellular compatibility.

A model of wound infection using Staphylococcus aureus was established. After a two-day treatment with composite hydrogels, the wounds were washed with PBS and then evenly covered with 50 µL of solution on the solid medium. After being grown for 24 h at 37 °C, they were photographed in order to be analyzed. Tissue was collected and used for Gram staining. As shown in Fig.S13, bacterial colonies at the wound site and within the tissues were significantly reduced following treatment with the composite hydrogels. The results showed that the composite hydrogel showed good antibacterial properties in vivo.

The healing of diabetic wounds was assessed to determine the potential application of composite hydrogels by applying them to diabetic model wounds and monitoring the healing process at various time periods. Fig. 8A depicts the animal experimental approach, which demonstrated the successful creation of the diabetes model using STZ injection. Blood glucose data showed a considerable increase in blood glucose concentration in the rats, as well as a noteworthy drop in body weight (Fig. S14). (Fig. S14). For the experiment, The rats were divided into 4 groups: PBS group (PBS), blank hydrogel group (HA-PBA/PVA), C-dots-loaded hydrogel group (HC-dots), and Zn/C-dots-loaded hydrogel group (H-Zn/C-dots), as shown in Fig. 8B and C. A noticeable difference in wound area appeared on the third day, and by the 14th day, the wounds in the H-Zn/C-dots treatment group were nearly fully healed, with a wound area of ≈ 9.19 %. The PBS, HA-PBA/PVA, and HC-dots groups had wound sizes of ≈ 75.18 %, 58.39 %, and 25.78 % respectively.

Wound healing was accompanied by a complex biological process that including inflammation, proliferation, and remodeling. To test the impact of the composite hydrogel on the above processes, histological alterations were observed to assess wound healing progress. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining revealed the presence of inflammatory cells in the PBS-treated skin tissues. In contrast, in the H-Zn/C-dots treatment group, few inflammatory cells were observed; some new epithelial cells and connective tissues were arranged more neatly, and some new structures resembling hair follicles and blood vessels were observed (Fig. 8D). Masson's staining was used to evaluate the effect of hydrogel treatment on collagen level in wound tissues. Collagen is the main component of the extracellular matrix of dermal tissues and plays a very important role in the process of wound healing. On the 14th day of treatment, the H-Zn/C-dots treatment group had significantly collagen deposition higher than in the other groups (Fig. 8E), followed by the H-Zn/C-dots, HA-PBA/PVA, and PBS groups. Furthermore, the H-Zn/C-dots treatment group showed distinct vascular lumen architecture. Vascular formation plays a vital role in wound healing, as it provides essential nourishment for the growth of granulation tissue [61]. Additionally, on the third day after treatment with HC-dots and H-Zn/C-dots, the expression of TNF-α in the wounds was much lower than in the other groups, according to the staining results of the inflammatory marker TNF-α (Fig. 9A-D). This indicated that both HC-dots and H-Zn/C-dots, with the H-Zn/C-dots treatment group having the most notable effect, may effectively reduce the early inflammatory response. Wounds in the HA-PBA/PVA and PBS groups had high TNF-α levels but no discernible CD31 expression on days 7 and 14 (Fig. 9E-G). This suggests that the wounds were still in a persistent inflammatory phase. Conversely, the H-Zn/C-dots group's wounds had a high CD31 expression and low TNF-α expression, indicating that they are in the proliferation and remodeling stage. It is worth noting that, while HC-dots also showed therapeutic outcomes, the group's treatment progressed at a slower rate than the H-Zn/C-dots group. As the above data demonstrate, H-Zn/C-dots have exceptional anti-inflammatory, collagen-repair, and angiogenesis properties that greatly aid in wound healing and tissue remodeling.

Inflammation-induced conditions impede the healing processes of damaged tissues, resulting in functional impairment. Although anti-inflammatory drugs are clinically available, they frequently have undesired side effects and are therefore deemed unsatisfactory. Here, a carbon dot-loaded zinc single-atom nanozyme (Zn/C-dots) was designed and prepared. Zn/C-dots have multi-enzyme activity that mimics the intracellular antioxidant defense system, resulting in antioxidant treatment and inflammation regulation by scavenging reactive oxygen species (ROS). Ultra-small carbon dots can be endocytosed by cells and accumulate in mitochondria. Zn/C-dots have high antibacterial activity in vitro (100 µg/mL) and promote endothelial cell migration and neovascularization. Zn/C-dots, as nanocatalytic medicines, hold significant potential for the treatment of diabetic wounds, as demonstrated by experiments on a diabetic rat model. They can enhance tissue remodeling, accelerate angiogenesis, promote collagen deposition, and reduce inflammation at the site of the wound. Furthermore, in vitro and in vivo experiments showed that Zn/C-dots are highly biocompatible. In conclusion, this study provides vital insights into the therapy of ROS-related illnesses by developing an effective nanodrug for diabetic wounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Declaration of generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
ce:section.ce:para: During the preparation of this work the authors used ChatGPT 4 in order to polish the language of the manuscript. After using this tool, the authors reviewed and edited the content as needed and take full responsibility for the content of the publication.
